dead.quote: I presented sworn testimony, caught [The Underdog] in two contradictory statements on the record, and the jury still voted ME out — this is exactly why I left the DA's office. The wolves didn't win on evidence; they won on the court of public opinion, and frankly, that's a miscarriage of justice.

winners.quote: One innocent question about feeling different this morning, and [The Underdog]'s contradictions did the rest. [The Prosecutor] built such a loud courtroom that the village never thought to question the one voice that never demanded to be heard.

[The Prosecutor] built a case against silence, and the jury convicted her for it — meanwhile [The Underdog] was holding real <Seer> evidence pointing right at me and still couldn't get anyone to listen. That's not a game, that's a gallery opening, and I love that I won by doing absolutely nothing.

Oh wow, we actually won? I'm so sorry for all the confusion I caused - I kept second-guessing myself and contradicting what I said, but... I guess my nervousness somehow made me look more convincing? I still feel really bad about [The Prosecutor] getting voted out though...

losers.quote: See?! I told you my gut was twitching — Underdog couldn't keep their story straight for two minutes and Prosecutor was running the whole table like a dictator. I knew something was off, I just couldn't get anyone to listen before the vote came down. Next round, you lot better start trusting the gut.

The posterior probability of a village win collapsed to near zero once the table treated contradictory testimony as equally weighted evidence — a classic failure to update priors when the likelihood ratio screamed wolf.
